articles.headline: Delhi CM Arvind Kejriwal Announces Resignation, Atishi Marlena Singh to Succeed
articles.explainer:
• Arvind Kejriwal announced his resignation as Chief Minister of Delhi, effective September 17, 2024.
• This decision comes amidst significant political developments in the National Capital Territory.
• Atishi Marlena Singh, a prominent figure in the Aam Aadmi Party (AAP) and a minister in the Delhi government, is set to succeed him.
• The move marks a significant leadership transition for the AAP in Delhi.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure
Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure, devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein
Appointment to various Constitutional posts, powers, functions and responsibilities of various Constitutional Bodies.
prelimsPoints: Arvind Kejriwal's resignation effective date: September 17, 2024.
Successor to Arvind Kejriwal: Atishi Marlena Singh.
Delhi is a Union Territory with a Legislative Assembly (Article 239AA of the Constitution).
The Chief Minister is appointed by the President on the recommendation of the Lieutenant Governor.
mainsPoints: Discuss the unique constitutional status of the National Capital Territory of Delhi and the powers of its Chief Minister vis-à-vis the Lieutenant Governor.
Analyze the implications of such leadership transitions on governance and political stability in Union Territories with legislatures.
Examine the role of regional parties like AAP in shaping state-level politics and policy.
articles.imageDescription: Arvind Kejriwal and Atishi Marlena Singh shaking hands, symbolizing the leadership transition in Delhi.
articles.trivia: Delhi's Chief Minister is technically appointed by the President, but practically, the President acts on the advice of the Lieutenant Governor, who in turn acts on the advice of the CM for most matters, except those reserved for the LG.

articles.headline: Can Painting 64 Yoginis Unlock the Secrets of the Divine Feminine?
articles.explainer:
• The article explores the historical and spiritual significance of the 64 Yogini temples in India.
• These temples are dedicated to a pantheon of powerful female deities, representing aspects of the Divine Feminine.
• The architecture and iconography of these circular, hypaethral (open-to-sky) temples hold unique tantric and yogic symbolism.
• Understanding the Yoginis provides insights into ancient Indian spiritual practices and the role of women in religious traditions.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times.
Salient features of Indian Society, Diversity of India.
prelimsPoints: 64 Yogini temples are associated with Tantric traditions.
They are typically circular and open-to-sky (hypaethral) in design.
Prominent 64 Yogini temples are found in Odisha (Hirapur, Ranipur-Jharial) and Madhya Pradesh (Mitawali/Morena, Khajuraho).
The Indian Parliament House's design is believed to be inspired by the 64 Yogini Temple in Mitawali, Morena.
mainsPoints: Discuss the socio-religious significance of the 64 Yogini cult in medieval India and its representation of the Divine Feminine.
Analyze the architectural uniqueness of the Yogini temples and their contribution to Indian art and architecture.
Evaluate how the study of such cults contributes to understanding the evolution of religious practices and the role of women in ancient and medieval Indian society.
articles.imageDescription: Stone carvings of Yoginis in a circular temple, showcasing ancient Indian artistry.
articles.trivia: The 64 Yoginis are often seen as manifestations of the goddess Durga or Kali, embodying immense power and mystical knowledge.

articles.headline: Nehru’s First Recruits: The Desi Hands that Shaped India’s Foreign Policy
articles.explainer:
• A new book, 'Nehru’s First Recruits', delves into the formative years of Indian diplomacy.
• It highlights the professional contributions, personalities, and challenges faced by India’s first diplomats.
• The book offers a rare perspective on how India's foreign policy was conceptualized and implemented post-independence.
• It underscores the foundational principles laid down by Jawaharlal Nehru and his team in shaping India's global stance.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighbourhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
Post-independence consolidation and reorganization within the country.
prelimsPoints: Jawaharlal Nehru was India's first Prime Minister and also held the portfolio of External Affairs.
India's early foreign policy was largely based on the principles of Non-Alignment and Panchsheel.
The Indian Foreign Service (IFS) was established shortly after independence.
Early Indian diplomats played a crucial role in establishing India's presence on the global stage amidst the Cold War.
mainsPoints: Evaluate the foundational principles of India's foreign policy under Jawaharlal Nehru and their relevance in contemporary international relations.
Discuss the challenges faced by India's early diplomats in a newly independent nation and a bipolar world order.
Analyze how the contributions of the first generation of diplomats shaped India's strategic autonomy and influence globally.
articles.imageDescription: Vintage photograph of Jawaharlal Nehru with early Indian diplomats.
articles.trivia: India was one of the founding members of the Non-Aligned Movement (NAM), which sought to maintain autonomy from the two major power blocs during the Cold War.

articles.headline: Siddhartha Deb’s 'Twilight Prisoners' Outlines Liberal Elite’s Contribution to Rise of Hindu Right
articles.explainer:
• Siddhartha Deb's book critically examines the role of India's liberal elite in the emergence and consolidation of the Hindu Right and the Narendra Modi regime.
• It challenges traditional narratives by suggesting that the elite's actions, or inactions, inadvertently paved the way for current political dynamics.
• The book prompts a re-evaluation of intellectual and political discourse surrounding contemporary Indian politics.
• It offers a nuanced perspective on the complex interplay between different ideological groups in India's political landscape.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure.
Salient features of Indian Society, Diversity of India.
Role of civil services in a democracy.
mainsPoints: Critically analyze the arguments presented in 'Twilight Prisoners' regarding the liberal elite's role in the rise of the Hindu Right in India.
Discuss the evolving definitions of 'liberal' and 'conservative' in the context of contemporary Indian political thought.
Examine the socio-political factors that contribute to the shifts in India's ideological landscape and the consolidation of political power.
articles.imageDescription: Book cover of 'Twilight Prisoners' by Siddhartha Deb, with a symbolic representation of India's political spectrum.
articles.trivia: The term 'Hindu Right' encompasses a range of organizations and ideologies that advocate for Hindu nationalist principles, often associated with the Sangh Parivar.

articles.headline: India's Economy Registers Robust Growth in Q1 FY25, Driven by Manufacturing and Services
articles.explainer:
• India's Gross Domestic Product (GDP) showed strong performance in the first quarter of Fiscal Year 2024-25.
• The growth was primarily propelled by robust activity in the manufacturing and services sectors.
• This positive trend indicates resilience in the domestic economy amidst global uncertainties.
• Government policies and increased domestic demand are cited as key contributors to this economic expansion.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Inclusive growth and issues arising from it.
Government Budgeting.

Major crops cropping patterns in various parts of the country, different types of irrigation and irrigation system storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers.
articles.source: Ministry of Statistics and Programme Implementation (Simulated)
prelimsPoints: Q1 FY25 refers to the period from April to June 2024.
GDP measures the total monetary or market value of all the finished goods and services produced within a country's borders in a specific time period.
Manufacturing and services are typically the largest contributors to India's GDP.
High GDP growth indicates economic expansion and potential for job creation.
mainsPoints: Analyze the key factors contributing to India's robust economic growth in Q1 FY25, particularly highlighting the roles of manufacturing and services.
Discuss the challenges and opportunities for sustaining this growth trajectory in the medium to long term, considering global economic headwinds.
Evaluate the effectiveness of current government policies in fostering economic growth and ensuring inclusive development.
articles.imageDescription: Upward trending graph showing India's GDP growth, with symbols representing industry and services.
articles.trivia: India is projected to be one of the fastest-growing major economies globally in the current decade.

articles.headline: Supreme Court Upholds Key Provisions of Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023
articles.explainer:
• The Supreme Court of India has delivered a landmark judgment, largely upholding the constitutionality of the Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDPA), 2023.
• The ruling clarifies the legal framework for data privacy and protection in India, addressing concerns raised by various petitions.
• The judgment emphasizes the balance between individual privacy rights and legitimate state interests, including national security.
• This decision provides legal certainty for businesses and individuals regarding data handling practices.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
Statutory, regulatory and various quasi-judicial bodies.
Role of IT in governance.
Cyber security.
prelimsPoints: The Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDPA) was enacted in 2023.
It aims to regulate the processing of digital personal data in India.
The Act recognizes the 'Right to Privacy' as a fundamental right (Puttaswamy judgment).
It establishes the Data Protection Board of India for enforcement.
Key provisions include consent requirements, data principal rights, and obligations of data fiduciaries.
mainsPoints: Examine the constitutional validity and key provisions of the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, as upheld by the Supreme Court.
Discuss the implications of this judgment for individual privacy rights, government surveillance, and the digital economy in India.
Compare India's data protection framework with global standards like GDPR, highlighting its unique features and challenges in implementation.
articles.imageDescription: Symbolic image of digital data flowing through a legal framework, with the Supreme Court emblem.
articles.trivia: The 'Right to Privacy' was declared a fundamental right under Article 21 of the Indian Constitution by the Supreme Court in the landmark K.S. Puttaswamy judgment in 2017.

articles.headline: India and France Conclude Strategic Dialogue, Focus on Defence, Space, and Indo-Pacific Cooperation
articles.explainer:
• India and France held their annual Strategic Dialogue, reinforcing their robust bilateral ties.
• Discussions focused on enhancing cooperation in critical sectors such as defence, space technology, and nuclear energy.
• Both nations reiterated their commitment to a free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific region, addressing regional security challenges.
• The dialogue underscored the growing convergence of strategic interests between New Delhi and Paris on global and regional issues.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate.
prelimsPoints: India and France share a long-standing strategic partnership established in 1998.
Key areas of cooperation include defence (e.g., Rafale jets, submarines), space (ISRO-CNES), nuclear energy, and counter-terrorism.
Both countries advocate for a rules-based international order and stability in the Indo-Pacific.
France is a permanent member of the UN Security Council and a key partner for India in Europe.
mainsPoints: Analyze the evolution of the India-France strategic partnership and its significance for India's foreign policy objectives.
Discuss how cooperation in defence, space, and the Indo-Pacific region strengthens the bilateral relationship and addresses shared security concerns.
Evaluate the role of India and France in shaping a multipolar world order and promoting strategic autonomy amidst geopolitical shifts.
articles.imageDescription: Flags of India and France displayed during a diplomatic meeting.
articles.trivia: India and France celebrated 25 years of their strategic partnership in 2023, marked by significant exchanges and agreements.

articles.headline: Government Launches New Scheme to Boost Green Hydrogen Production and Adoption
articles.explainer:
• The Indian government has unveiled a comprehensive new scheme aimed at accelerating green hydrogen production and its adoption across various sectors.
• The initiative includes incentives for manufacturing electrolysers and producing green hydrogen, alongside demand creation mechanisms.
• This move is part of India's broader National Green Hydrogen Mission, targeting energy independence and decarbonization.
• The scheme is expected to attract significant investments and create job opportunities in the renewable energy sector.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Infrastructure: Energy.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Investment models.
articles.source: Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (Simulated)
prelimsPoints: Green hydrogen is produced by electrolysis of water using renewable energy sources.
India launched the National Green Hydrogen Mission in January 2023.
The mission aims to make India a global hub for green hydrogen production and export.
Electrolysers are key components for producing green hydrogen.
Applications include industrial feedstock, transportation fuel, and energy storage.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of green hydrogen for India's energy security and climate change mitigation goals.
Analyze the key components and expected outcomes of the government's new scheme to boost green hydrogen production and adoption.
Identify the challenges in scaling up green hydrogen production and infrastructure in India and suggest policy measures to overcome them.
articles.imageDescription: A futuristic image of an electrolyser plant producing green hydrogen with wind turbines and solar panels in the background.
articles.trivia: India aims to achieve an annual green hydrogen production capacity of 5 million metric tonnes by 2030 under its National Green Hydrogen Mission.

articles.headline: ISRO Prepares for Venus Orbiter Mission 'Shukrayaan-1', Aims for 2028 Launch
articles.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) is actively preparing for its ambitious Venus Orbiter Mission, named 'Shukrayaan-1'.
• The mission aims to study the atmosphere and surface of Venus, focusing on its geological and atmospheric evolution.
• 'Shukrayaan-1' is tentatively scheduled for a 2028 launch, following extensive developmental work on scientific payloads.
• This mission signifies India's expanding capabilities in planetary exploration beyond Mars and the Moon.
gsSubTopics: Achievements of Indians in science & technology; Indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Awareness in the fields of Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights.
prelimsPoints: ISRO's Venus mission is named 'Shukrayaan-1'.
The primary objective is to study Venus's atmosphere and surface.
Venus is often called Earth's 'sister planet' due to similar size and mass, but has a vastly different atmosphere.
The mission will carry scientific payloads developed by Indian and international institutions.
Previous successful planetary missions by ISRO include Chandrayaan (Moon) and Mangalyaan (Mars).
mainsPoints: Discuss the scientific objectives of ISRO's 'Shukrayaan-1' mission and its potential contributions to understanding planetary evolution.
Analyze the challenges involved in a Venus mission, considering the planet's extreme atmospheric conditions.
Evaluate the significance of India's expanding space exploration program for its scientific capabilities, technological advancements, and international standing.
articles.imageDescription: Artistic rendering of the Shukrayaan-1 orbiter approaching Venus, with its thick atmosphere visible.
articles.trivia: Venus has the hottest surface temperature of any planet in our solar system, primarily due to a runaway greenhouse effect caused by its dense carbon dioxide atmosphere.

articles.headline: Ismail Darbesh’s 'Talashnama' Offers Rare View of Rural Muslim Life, Reclaiming Nuance
articles.explainer:
• Ismail Darbesh’s 'Talashnama' provides an enriching and authentic portrayal of the life of rural Muslims in India.
• The book aims to reclaim the community's right to nuance, moving beyond stereotypical representations.
• It delves into the daily struggles, cultural practices, and socio-economic realities often overlooked in mainstream narratives.
• The work contributes significantly to understanding the diversity and complexities within Indian society.
gsSubTopics: Salient features of Indian Society, Diversity of India.
Social empowerment, communalism, regionalism & secularism.
Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature.
mainsPoints: Discuss how literary works like 'Talashnama' contribute to a more nuanced understanding of minority communities in India, challenging prevalent stereotypes.
Examine the socio-economic conditions and cultural practices of rural Muslim communities in India, highlighting their diversity.
Analyze the importance of literature in fostering social empathy and promoting inclusive narratives within a diverse society.
articles.imageDescription: A book cover depicting scenes from rural India, with subtle cultural motifs.
articles.trivia: India has the third-largest Muslim population in the world, with significant diversity in language, culture, and practices across different regions.

articles.headline: Gigi Ganguly’s 'Biopeculiar' Heightens Awareness of Climate-Change-Threatened Natural World
articles.explainer:
• Gigi Ganguly’s collection of speculative fiction, 'Biopeculiar', explores themes of climate change and environmental degradation.
• The book uses imaginative narratives to heighten readers' awareness of the climate-change-threatened natural world.
• It delves into potential future scenarios and the impact of human actions on ecosystems and biodiversity.
• 'Biopeculiar' contributes to the growing genre of 'cli-fi' (climate fiction), urging reflection and action on environmental issues.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Disaster and disaster management.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
mainsPoints: Discuss the role of speculative fiction, such as 'Biopeculiar', in raising public awareness and promoting dialogue on critical environmental issues like climate change.
Analyze how literary works can effectively communicate the long-term impacts of human activities on the natural world and inspire sustainable practices.
Examine the concept of the Anthropocene and its implications for understanding the relationship between humanity and the Earth's ecosystems.
articles.imageDescription: A surreal book cover depicting distorted natural elements, symbolizing environmental change.
articles.trivia: The term 'Anthropocene' was popularized by Nobel laureate Paul Crutzen, suggesting a new geological epoch dominated by human activity.

articles.headline: 'Whispers of the Unseen' by S. Beena Unnikrishnan: A Journey of Discovery Through Art and Spirituality
articles.explainer:
• S. Beena Unnikrishnan's 'Whispers of the Unseen' is described as a personal journey of discovery through art.
• The book challenges traditional ideas of spirituality and the perception of time, offering a fresh perspective.
• It explores the intersection of artistic expression and inner spiritual experience.
• The work encourages readers to reflect on their own understanding of existence, art, and the unseen dimensions of life.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature.
Salient features of Indian Society.
articles.imageDescription: An abstract painting with spiritual motifs, evoking a sense of mystery and introspection.
articles.trivia: Many ancient Indian philosophies, such as Yoga and Vedanta, delve deeply into the nature of consciousness and the perception of reality beyond the physical senses.

articles.headline: 'Stories for the Anthropocene': Reflecting on Humanity's Mark on Earth
articles.explainer:
• This collection of stories explores themes related to the Anthropocene, the proposed geological epoch defined by human impact on Earth.
• It brings together narratives that reflect on environmental changes, ecological crises, and the future of human-nature interaction.
• The anthology serves as a literary response to the challenges posed by climate change and biodiversity loss.
• It encourages readers to critically examine humanity's responsibility towards the planet.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Disaster and disaster management.
mainsPoints: Discuss how the concept of the Anthropocene fundamentally alters our understanding of human-environment interactions and global challenges.
Analyze the role of literature and storytelling in disseminating complex scientific concepts like climate change and fostering public engagement.
Examine the ethical considerations and responsibilities that arise from humanity's significant impact on Earth's systems.
articles.imageDescription: Abstract artwork depicting human figures interacting with a changing global landscape.
articles.trivia: While the term 'Anthropocene' is widely used, it is not yet formally adopted as a geological epoch by the International Commission on Stratigraphy (ICS).

articles.headline: 'Portrait of a People': Exploring Cultural Narratives and Identity Through Art
articles.explainer:
• The article reviews 'Portrait of a People', a work that delves into various cultural narratives and the formation of identity.
• It examines how art and storytelling reflect and shape the collective consciousness of a community or nation.
• The book highlights the richness and diversity of human experiences expressed through different forms of artistic creation.
• It offers insights into the societal values, historical contexts, and individual perspectives that contribute to a 'portrait' of a people.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature.
Salient features of Indian Society, Diversity of India.
articles.imageDescription: A collage of diverse faces and cultural symbols, representing a 'portrait of a people'.
articles.trivia: India's 'unity in diversity' is often expressed through its myriad art forms, languages, and festivals, each contributing to a unique cultural mosaic.

articles.headline: 'Perfume': A Tamil Story in Translation by Ashokamitran, Highlighting Regional Literature
articles.explainer:
• The article reviews the translated version of Ashokamitran's Tamil story, 'Perfume', translated by the author himself.
• It highlights the importance of literary translations in bringing regional Indian literature to a wider audience.
• The story likely offers a glimpse into Tamil culture, societal nuances, and human experiences from a regional perspective.
• Such translations enrich the national literary landscape and foster inter-cultural understanding.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature.
Salient features of Indian Society, Diversity of India.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of literary translations in promoting linguistic diversity and fostering national integration in India.
Analyze how regional literature, through translation, contributes to a broader understanding of India's cultural tapestry and varied socio-economic realities.
Evaluate the challenges and benefits of authors translating their own works into other languages.
articles.imageDescription: Book cover of 'Perfume', with Tamil script and a subtle artistic design.
articles.trivia: Ashokamitran (born J. Thyagarajan) was a prolific Tamil writer known for his realistic portrayal of middle-class life and his keen observation of human nature.

articles.headline: GST Council Convenes to Discuss Rate Rationalization and Compliance Reforms
articles.explainer:
• The Goods and Services Tax (GST) Council held a crucial meeting to address various aspects of the indirect tax regime.
• Key agenda items included rate rationalization for certain goods and services, aiming for simplification and efficiency.
• Discussions also focused on enhancing compliance mechanisms and leveraging technology for better tax administration.
• The council sought to balance revenue generation with promoting economic growth and easing the burden on businesses.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Government Budgeting.
Inclusive growth and issues arising from it.
prelimsPoints: The GST Council is a constitutional body (Article 279A).
It is chaired by the Union Finance Minister and includes state finance ministers.
The council makes recommendations on GST rates, exemptions, thresholds, and rules.
Rate rationalization aims to simplify the existing multi-slab GST structure.
Enhanced compliance measures include improved e-invoicing and data analytics.
mainsPoints: Evaluate the effectiveness of the GST regime in India since its implementation, particularly in terms of revenue buoyancy and ease of doing business.
Discuss the challenges associated with GST rate rationalization and the implications of proposed reforms on various sectors of the economy.
Analyze the role of the GST Council as a unique federal institution in resolving fiscal issues between the Union and States.
articles.imageDescription: A gavel and tax documents on a table, symbolizing GST council decisions.
articles.trivia: GST was implemented in India on July 1, 2017, as a comprehensive indirect tax reform, replacing multiple cascading taxes levied by the central and state governments.

articles.headline: Review Meeting Held on National Education Policy 2020 Implementation Progress
articles.explainer:
• A high-level review meeting was conducted to assess the progress of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 implementation.
• The discussion focused on the reforms introduced in school education, higher education, and teacher training.
• Emphasis was placed on integrating technology, promoting multidisciplinary learning, and skill development.
• The review aimed to identify bottlenecks and accelerate the rollout of NEP initiatives across states and educational institutions.
gsSubTopics: Development processes and the development industry —the role of NGOs, SHGs, various groups and associations, donors, charities, institutional and other stakeholders.
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
prelimsPoints: The National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 replaced the 1986 policy.
It proposes significant structural changes from school to higher education.
Key pillars include access, equity, quality, affordability, and accountability.
It recommends a 5+3+3+4 curricular structure for school education.
Emphasis on multidisciplinary learning, critical thinking, and vocational education.
mainsPoints: Critically evaluate the key reforms proposed by the National Education Policy 2020 and their potential to transform India's education system.
Discuss the challenges encountered in the implementation of NEP 2020, particularly concerning federal cooperation, funding, and teacher preparedness.
Analyze how NEP 2020 aims to align India's education system with the demands of the 21st-century knowledge economy and global standards.
articles.imageDescription: Students learning in a modern classroom setting, symbolizing educational reform.
articles.trivia: NEP 2020 aims to increase the Gross Enrolment Ratio (GER) in higher education to 50% by 2035.

articles.headline: Monsoon Withdrawal Commences from Northwest India, Concerns Over Uneven Rainfall Distribution
articles.explainer:
• The India Meteorological Department (IMD) announced the commencement of monsoon withdrawal from parts of Northwest India.
• While some regions received abundant rainfall, others experienced deficits, leading to concerns about agricultural output.
• The uneven distribution of monsoon rains has implications for kharif crops and water reservoir levels.
• Farmers in affected areas may face challenges, prompting calls for government intervention and drought mitigation strategies.
gsSubTopics: Salient features of world’s physical geography.

Distribution of key natural resources across the world (including South Asia and the Indian subcontinent); factors responsible for the location of primary, secondary, and tertiary sector industries in various parts of the world (including India).

Important Geophysical phenomena such as earthquakes, Tsunami, Volcanic activity, cyclone etc., geographical features and their location-changes in critical geographical features (including water-bodies and ice-caps) and in flora and fauna and the effects of such changes.
prelimsPoints: Monsoon withdrawal typically begins from Northwest India in mid-September.
The Southwest Monsoon brings most of India's annual rainfall.
Kharif crops (e.g., rice, maize, pulses) are sown during the monsoon season.
Uneven rainfall can lead to agricultural distress, impacting food security and rural incomes.
The India Meteorological Department (IMD) monitors monsoon patterns and issues forecasts.
mainsPoints: Analyze the factors influencing the withdrawal of the Indian monsoon and its implications for agricultural productivity and water resources in different regions.
Discuss the challenges posed by uneven monsoon rainfall distribution for Indian agriculture and the strategies adopted by the government to mitigate their impact.
Examine the long-term trends in monsoon variability and their potential linkage to climate change, and suggest measures for climate-resilient agriculture.
articles.imageDescription: A map of India showing rainfall distribution, with some areas marked dry and others wet.
articles.trivia: The 'Break in Monsoon' is a common phenomenon where rainfall decreases significantly over central India for a week or more during the monsoon season.

articles.headline: BCCI Finalizes New IPL Media Rights Deal, Signals Major Revenue Boost for Cricket
articles.explainer:
• The Board of Control for Cricket in India (BCCI) has concluded a new round of media rights sales for the Indian Premier League (IPL).
• The deal, spanning several years, is expected to generate unprecedented revenue, making IPL one of the most lucrative sports leagues globally.
• This significant financial injection will further bolster the development of cricket infrastructure and talent in India.
• The new rights package covers both television and digital streaming, reflecting evolving media consumption patterns.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Inclusive growth and issues arising from it.
keyTerms: Board of Control for Cricket in India (BCCI)
prelimsPoints: The IPL is a professional Twenty20 cricket league in India.
BCCI is the governing body for cricket in India.
Media rights deals are a major source of revenue for sports leagues.
The IPL's valuation has consistently grown since its inception in 2008.
Digital streaming platforms are increasingly acquiring sports broadcasting rights.
mainsPoints: Analyze the economic impact of the Indian Premier League (IPL) on the Indian sports industry and broader economy, including revenue generation and employment.
Discuss how the evolving landscape of media rights, particularly the shift towards digital platforms, is reshaping the business model of major sports leagues.
Evaluate the role of sports leagues like IPL in promoting national identity, cultural engagement, and soft power for India on the global stage.
articles.imageDescription: An IPL trophy alongside a blurred cricket stadium, symbolizing commercial success in sports.
articles.trivia: The IPL is often cited as the world's richest cricket league and one of the most valuable sports properties globally.

highlights: Delhi CM Arvind Kejriwal announces resignation, Atishi Marlena Singh to succeed, marking a major political transition.
India's economy records robust growth in Q1 FY25, driven by strong performance in manufacturing and services sectors.
Supreme Court upholds key provisions of the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, providing clarity on data privacy laws.
Government launches new scheme to boost green hydrogen production and adoption, aligning with National Green Hydrogen Mission goals.
India and France conclude Strategic Dialogue, reinforcing cooperation in defence, space, and Indo-Pacific security.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to the National Capital Territory of Delhi, consider the following statements:
1. The Chief Minister of Delhi is appointed by the President on the recommendation of the Lieutenant Governor.
2. Delhi has a unique status as a Union Territory with a Legislative Assembly under Article 239AA of the Constitution.
3. The Council of Ministers in Delhi is collectively responsible to the Parliament of India.
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct. The Chief Minister of Delhi is appointed by the President on the recommendation of the Lieutenant Governor. Statement 2 is correct. Article 239AA grants Delhi a special status as a Union Territory with a Legislative Assembly. Statement 3 is incorrect. The Council of Ministers in Delhi is collectively responsible to the Legislative Assembly of Delhi, not the Parliament of India.

prelimsMocks.question:
Which of the following statements about 64 Yogini temples in India is/are correct?
1. They are typically circular and open-to-sky (hypaethral) in their architectural design.
2. The design of the Indian Parliament House is believed to be inspired by one such temple.
3. They are primarily dedicated to Vaishnavite deities.
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct; 64 Yogini temples are known for their distinct circular, hypaethral architecture. Statement 2 is correct; the 64 Yogini Temple in Mitawali, Morena, is often cited as an inspiration for the Parliament House. Statement 3 is incorrect; these temples are primarily associated with Tantric cults and the worship of various manifestations of the Divine Feminine (Shakti), not exclusively Vaishnavite deities.

prelimsMocks.question:
Consider the following statements regarding the Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDPA), 2023:
1. It recognizes the 'Right to Privacy' as a fundamental right.
2. It establishes the Data Protection Board of India for enforcing its provisions.
3. It applies only to the processing of digital personal data within India.
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct; the DPDPA is rooted in the recognition of the Right to Privacy as a fundamental right by the Supreme Court. Statement 2 is correct; the Act provides for the establishment of the Data Protection Board of India. Statement 3 is incorrect; the Act has extra-territorial applicability, meaning it can apply to the processing of personal data outside India if it relates to offering goods or services to data principals within India.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to the National Green Hydrogen Mission, consider the following statements:
1. Its primary objective is to make India a global hub for green hydrogen production and export.
2. Green hydrogen is produced by electrolysis of water using renewable energy sources.
3. The mission aims to achieve an annual green hydrogen production capacity of 10 million metric tonnes by 2030.
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct; making India a global hub for green hydrogen is a key goal. Statement 2 is correct; this is the definition of green hydrogen. Statement 3 is incorrect; the mission aims for an annual green hydrogen production capacity of 5 million metric tonnes by 2030, not 10 million.

prelimsMocks.question:
Which of the following principles largely guided India's foreign policy during the Jawaharlal Nehru era?
1. Non-Alignment
2. Panchsheel
3. Bilateralism with Western Blocs only
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statements 1 and 2 are correct. Non-Alignment and Panchsheel were the cornerstones of India's foreign policy under Nehru, emphasizing independence from superpower blocs and peaceful coexistence. Statement 3 is incorrect. India pursued a policy of non-alignment, engaging with both Western and Eastern blocs without aligning exclusively with either. While bilateral relations with Western blocs existed, they were not the sole focus.

mainsMocks.question: Critically analyze the unique constitutional status of the National Capital Territory of Delhi. Discuss the implications of frequent political transitions and power dynamics between the elected government and the Lieutenant Governor on governance in Delhi. (15 marks)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure, devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce Delhi's special status (Article 239AA). Explain the dual control mechanism (elected government vs. LG). Discuss challenges like frequent power tussles, impact on policy implementation, and political stability. Conclude with suggestions for better cooperative federalism.

mainsMocks.question: The Supreme Court's upholding of the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, marks a significant step towards safeguarding privacy in the digital age. Discuss the key provisions of the Act and critically evaluate its effectiveness in balancing individual privacy rights with national security and legitimate state interests. (15 marks)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation. GS3: Cyber security.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce the DPDPA 2023 and the SC ruling. Detail key provisions (consent, data principal rights, data fiduciary obligations, Data Protection Board). Analyze the balance between privacy and state interests, highlighting strengths (e.g., consent framework) and potential weaknesses (e.g., exemptions for government agencies). Conclude with its significance for India's digital future.

mainsMocks.question: India's foreign policy, shaped by its initial architects like Jawaharlal Nehru, has evolved significantly. Discuss the foundational principles of India's foreign policy during the post-independence era and analyze how these principles continue to influence India's strategic autonomy and engagement in a multipolar world today. (15 marks)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: India and its neighbourhood- relations; Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests; Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce Nehru's role and the context of post-independence. Explain foundational principles like Non-Alignment, Panchsheel, anti-colonialism, and promotion of peace. Discuss how these principles, though adapted, still inform India's pursuit of strategic autonomy, engagement with multiple powers, and stance on global issues. Conclude on the continuity and change in India's foreign policy.

quickBytes.text: Arvind Kejriwal's resignation as Delhi CM is effective Sep 17, 2024.
Atishi Marlena Singh is set to become the new Chief Minister of Delhi.
The 64 Yogini Temple in Mitawali, Morena, inspired India's Parliament House design.
India aims for 5 million tonnes of green hydrogen production by 2030.
The Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDPA) was enacted in 2023.
ISRO's Venus Orbiter Mission is named 'Shukrayaan-1', planned for 2028.
The 'Anthropocene' refers to a geological epoch dominated by human activity.
Non-Alignment was a cornerstone of India's foreign policy under Nehru.
The GST Council is a constitutional body established under Article 279A.
NEP 2020 proposes a 5+3+3+4 curricular structure for school education.

GS1.headline: Can Painting 64 Yoginis Unlock the Secrets of the Divine Feminine?
GS1.explainer:
• The article explores the historical and spiritual significance of the 64 Yogini temples in India.
• These temples are dedicated to a pantheon of powerful female deities, representing aspects of the Divine Feminine.
• The architecture and iconography of these circular, hypaethral (open-to-sky) temples hold unique tantric and yogic symbolism.
• Understanding the Yoginis provides insights into ancient Indian spiritual practices and the role of women in religious traditions.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times.
Salient features of Indian Society, Diversity of India.
prelimsPoints: 64 Yogini temples are associated with Tantric traditions.
They are typically circular and open-to-sky (hypaethral) in design.
Prominent 64 Yogini temples are found in Odisha (Hirapur, Ranipur-Jharial) and Madhya Pradesh (Mitawali/Morena, Khajuraho).
The Indian Parliament House's design is believed to be inspired by the 64 Yogini Temple in Mitawali, Morena.
mainsPoints: Discuss the socio-religious significance of the 64 Yogini cult in medieval India and its representation of the Divine Feminine.
Analyze the architectural uniqueness of the Yogini temples and their contribution to Indian art and architecture.
Evaluate how the study of such cults contributes to understanding the evolution of religious practices and the role of women in ancient and medieval Indian society.
GS1.imageDescription: Stone carvings of Yoginis in a circular temple, showcasing ancient Indian artistry.
GS1.trivia: The 64 Yoginis are often seen as manifestations of the goddess Durga or Kali, embodying immense power and mystical knowledge.

GS1.headline: Ismail Darbesh’s 'Talashnama' Offers Rare View of Rural Muslim Life, Reclaiming Nuance
GS1.explainer:
• Ismail Darbesh’s 'Talashnama' provides an enriching and authentic portrayal of the life of rural Muslims in India.
• The book aims to reclaim the community's right to nuance, moving beyond stereotypical representations.
• It delves into the daily struggles, cultural practices, and socio-economic realities often overlooked in mainstream narratives.
• The work contributes significantly to understanding the diversity and complexities within Indian society.
gsSubTopics: Salient features of Indian Society, Diversity of India.
Social empowerment, communalism, regionalism & secularism.
Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature.
mainsPoints: Discuss how literary works like 'Talashnama' contribute to a more nuanced understanding of minority communities in India, challenging prevalent stereotypes.
Examine the socio-economic conditions and cultural practices of rural Muslim communities in India, highlighting their diversity.
Analyze the importance of literature in fostering social empathy and promoting inclusive narratives within a diverse society.
GS1.imageDescription: A book cover depicting scenes from rural India, with subtle cultural motifs.
GS1.trivia: India has the third-largest Muslim population in the world, with significant diversity in language, culture, and practices across different regions.

GS1.headline: 'Whispers of the Unseen' by S. Beena Unnikrishnan: A Journey of Discovery Through Art and Spirituality
GS1.explainer:
• S. Beena Unnikrishnan's 'Whispers of the Unseen' is described as a personal journey of discovery through art.
• The book challenges traditional ideas of spirituality and the perception of time, offering a fresh perspective.
• It explores the intersection of artistic expression and inner spiritual experience.
• The work encourages readers to reflect on their own understanding of existence, art, and the unseen dimensions of life.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature.
Salient features of Indian Society.
GS1.imageDescription: An abstract painting with spiritual motifs, evoking a sense of mystery and introspection.
GS1.trivia: Many ancient Indian philosophies, such as Yoga and Vedanta, delve deeply into the nature of consciousness and the perception of reality beyond the physical senses.

GS1.headline: 'Portrait of a People': Exploring Cultural Narratives and Identity Through Art
GS1.explainer:
• The article reviews 'Portrait of a People', a work that delves into various cultural narratives and the formation of identity.
• It examines how art and storytelling reflect and shape the collective consciousness of a community or nation.
• The book highlights the richness and diversity of human experiences expressed through different forms of artistic creation.
• It offers insights into the societal values, historical contexts, and individual perspectives that contribute to a 'portrait' of a people.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature.
Salient features of Indian Society, Diversity of India.
GS1.imageDescription: A collage of diverse faces and cultural symbols, representing a 'portrait of a people'.
GS1.trivia: India's 'unity in diversity' is often expressed through its myriad art forms, languages, and festivals, each contributing to a unique cultural mosaic.

GS1.headline: 'Perfume': A Tamil Story in Translation by Ashokamitran, Highlighting Regional Literature
GS1.explainer:
• The article reviews the translated version of Ashokamitran's Tamil story, 'Perfume', translated by the author himself.
• It highlights the importance of literary translations in bringing regional Indian literature to a wider audience.
• The story likely offers a glimpse into Tamil culture, societal nuances, and human experiences from a regional perspective.
• Such translations enrich the national literary landscape and foster inter-cultural understanding.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature.
Salient features of Indian Society, Diversity of India.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of literary translations in promoting linguistic diversity and fostering national integration in India.
Analyze how regional literature, through translation, contributes to a broader understanding of India's cultural tapestry and varied socio-economic realities.
Evaluate the challenges and benefits of authors translating their own works into other languages.
GS1.imageDescription: Book cover of 'Perfume', with Tamil script and a subtle artistic design.
GS1.trivia: Ashokamitran (born J. Thyagarajan) was a prolific Tamil writer known for his realistic portrayal of middle-class life and his keen observation of human nature.

GS1.headline: Monsoon Withdrawal Commences from Northwest India, Concerns Over Uneven Rainfall Distribution
GS1.explainer:
• The India Meteorological Department (IMD) announced the commencement of monsoon withdrawal from parts of Northwest India.
• While some regions received abundant rainfall, others experienced deficits, leading to concerns about agricultural output.
• The uneven distribution of monsoon rains has implications for kharif crops and water reservoir levels.
• Farmers in affected areas may face challenges, prompting calls for government intervention and drought mitigation strategies.
gsSubTopics: Salient features of world’s physical geography.

Distribution of key natural resources across the world (including South Asia and the Indian subcontinent); factors responsible for the location of primary, secondary, and tertiary sector industries in various parts of the world (including India).

Important Geophysical phenomena such as earthquakes, Tsunami, Volcanic activity, cyclone etc., geographical features and their location-changes in critical geographical features (including water-bodies and ice-caps) and in flora and fauna and the effects of such changes.
prelimsPoints: Monsoon withdrawal typically begins from Northwest India in mid-September.
The Southwest Monsoon brings most of India's annual rainfall.
Kharif crops (e.g., rice, maize, pulses) are sown during the monsoon season.
Uneven rainfall can lead to agricultural distress, impacting food security and rural incomes.
The India Meteorological Department (IMD) monitors monsoon patterns and issues forecasts.
mainsPoints: Analyze the factors influencing the withdrawal of the Indian monsoon and its implications for agricultural productivity and water resources in different regions.
Discuss the challenges posed by uneven monsoon rainfall distribution for Indian agriculture and the strategies adopted by the government to mitigate their impact.
Examine the long-term trends in monsoon variability and their potential linkage to climate change, and suggest measures for climate-resilient agriculture.
GS1.imageDescription: A map of India showing rainfall distribution, with some areas marked dry and others wet.
GS1.trivia: The 'Break in Monsoon' is a common phenomenon where rainfall decreases significantly over central India for a week or more during the monsoon season.

GS2.headline: Delhi CM Arvind Kejriwal Announces Resignation, Atishi Marlena Singh to Succeed
GS2.explainer:
• Arvind Kejriwal announced his resignation as Chief Minister of Delhi, effective September 17, 2024.
• This decision comes amidst significant political developments in the National Capital Territory.
• Atishi Marlena Singh, a prominent figure in the Aam Aadmi Party (AAP) and a minister in the Delhi government, is set to succeed him.
• The move marks a significant leadership transition for the AAP in Delhi.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure
Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure, devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein
Appointment to various Constitutional posts, powers, functions and responsibilities of various Constitutional Bodies.
prelimsPoints: Arvind Kejriwal's resignation effective date: September 17, 2024.
Successor to Arvind Kejriwal: Atishi Marlena Singh.
Delhi is a Union Territory with a Legislative Assembly (Article 239AA of the Constitution).
The Chief Minister is appointed by the President on the recommendation of the Lieutenant Governor.
mainsPoints: Discuss the unique constitutional status of the National Capital Territory of Delhi and the powers of its Chief Minister vis-à-vis the Lieutenant Governor.
Analyze the implications of such leadership transitions on governance and political stability in Union Territories with legislatures.
Examine the role of regional parties like AAP in shaping state-level politics and policy.
GS2.imageDescription: Arvind Kejriwal and Atishi Marlena Singh shaking hands, symbolizing the leadership transition in Delhi.
GS2.trivia: Delhi's Chief Minister is technically appointed by the President, but practically, the President acts on the advice of the Lieutenant Governor, who in turn acts on the advice of the CM for most matters, except those reserved for the LG.

GS2.headline: Nehru’s First Recruits: The Desi Hands that Shaped India’s Foreign Policy
GS2.explainer:
• A new book, 'Nehru’s First Recruits', delves into the formative years of Indian diplomacy.
• It highlights the professional contributions, personalities, and challenges faced by India’s first diplomats.
• The book offers a rare perspective on how India's foreign policy was conceptualized and implemented post-independence.
• It underscores the foundational principles laid down by Jawaharlal Nehru and his team in shaping India's global stance.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighbourhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
Post-independence consolidation and reorganization within the country.
prelimsPoints: Jawaharlal Nehru was India's first Prime Minister and also held the portfolio of External Affairs.
India's early foreign policy was largely based on the principles of Non-Alignment and Panchsheel.
The Indian Foreign Service (IFS) was established shortly after independence.
Early Indian diplomats played a crucial role in establishing India's presence on the global stage amidst the Cold War.
mainsPoints: Evaluate the foundational principles of India's foreign policy under Jawaharlal Nehru and their relevance in contemporary international relations.
Discuss the challenges faced by India's early diplomats in a newly independent nation and a bipolar world order.
Analyze how the contributions of the first generation of diplomats shaped India's strategic autonomy and influence globally.
GS2.imageDescription: Vintage photograph of Jawaharlal Nehru with early Indian diplomats.
GS2.trivia: India was one of the founding members of the Non-Aligned Movement (NAM), which sought to maintain autonomy from the two major power blocs during the Cold War.

GS2.headline: Siddhartha Deb’s 'Twilight Prisoners' Outlines Liberal Elite’s Contribution to Rise of Hindu Right
GS2.explainer:
• Siddhartha Deb's book critically examines the role of India's liberal elite in the emergence and consolidation of the Hindu Right and the Narendra Modi regime.
• It challenges traditional narratives by suggesting that the elite's actions, or inactions, inadvertently paved the way for current political dynamics.
• The book prompts a re-evaluation of intellectual and political discourse surrounding contemporary Indian politics.
• It offers a nuanced perspective on the complex interplay between different ideological groups in India's political landscape.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure.
Salient features of Indian Society, Diversity of India.
Role of civil services in a democracy.
mainsPoints: Critically analyze the arguments presented in 'Twilight Prisoners' regarding the liberal elite's role in the rise of the Hindu Right in India.
Discuss the evolving definitions of 'liberal' and 'conservative' in the context of contemporary Indian political thought.
Examine the socio-political factors that contribute to the shifts in India's ideological landscape and the consolidation of political power.
GS2.imageDescription: Book cover of 'Twilight Prisoners' by Siddhartha Deb, with a symbolic representation of India's political spectrum.
GS2.trivia: The term 'Hindu Right' encompasses a range of organizations and ideologies that advocate for Hindu nationalist principles, often associated with the Sangh Parivar.

GS2.headline: Supreme Court Upholds Key Provisions of Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023
GS2.explainer:
• The Supreme Court of India has delivered a landmark judgment, largely upholding the constitutionality of the Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDPA), 2023.
• The ruling clarifies the legal framework for data privacy and protection in India, addressing concerns raised by various petitions.
• The judgment emphasizes the balance between individual privacy rights and legitimate state interests, including national security.
• This decision provides legal certainty for businesses and individuals regarding data handling practices.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
Statutory, regulatory and various quasi-judicial bodies.
Role of IT in governance.
Cyber security.
prelimsPoints: The Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDPA) was enacted in 2023.
It aims to regulate the processing of digital personal data in India.
The Act recognizes the 'Right to Privacy' as a fundamental right (Puttaswamy judgment).
It establishes the Data Protection Board of India for enforcement.
Key provisions include consent requirements, data principal rights, and obligations of data fiduciaries.
mainsPoints: Examine the constitutional validity and key provisions of the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, as upheld by the Supreme Court.
Discuss the implications of this judgment for individual privacy rights, government surveillance, and the digital economy in India.
Compare India's data protection framework with global standards like GDPR, highlighting its unique features and challenges in implementation.
GS2.imageDescription: Symbolic image of digital data flowing through a legal framework, with the Supreme Court emblem.
GS2.trivia: The 'Right to Privacy' was declared a fundamental right under Article 21 of the Indian Constitution by the Supreme Court in the landmark K.S. Puttaswamy judgment in 2017.

GS2.headline: India and France Conclude Strategic Dialogue, Focus on Defence, Space, and Indo-Pacific Cooperation
GS2.explainer:
• India and France held their annual Strategic Dialogue, reinforcing their robust bilateral ties.
• Discussions focused on enhancing cooperation in critical sectors such as defence, space technology, and nuclear energy.
• Both nations reiterated their commitment to a free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific region, addressing regional security challenges.
• The dialogue underscored the growing convergence of strategic interests between New Delhi and Paris on global and regional issues.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate.
prelimsPoints: India and France share a long-standing strategic partnership established in 1998.
Key areas of cooperation include defence (e.g., Rafale jets, submarines), space (ISRO-CNES), nuclear energy, and counter-terrorism.
Both countries advocate for a rules-based international order and stability in the Indo-Pacific.
France is a permanent member of the UN Security Council and a key partner for India in Europe.
mainsPoints: Analyze the evolution of the India-France strategic partnership and its significance for India's foreign policy objectives.
Discuss how cooperation in defence, space, and the Indo-Pacific region strengthens the bilateral relationship and addresses shared security concerns.
Evaluate the role of India and France in shaping a multipolar world order and promoting strategic autonomy amidst geopolitical shifts.
GS2.imageDescription: Flags of India and France displayed during a diplomatic meeting.
GS2.trivia: India and France celebrated 25 years of their strategic partnership in 2023, marked by significant exchanges and agreements.

GS2.headline: Review Meeting Held on National Education Policy 2020 Implementation Progress
GS2.explainer:
• A high-level review meeting was conducted to assess the progress of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 implementation.
• The discussion focused on the reforms introduced in school education, higher education, and teacher training.
• Emphasis was placed on integrating technology, promoting multidisciplinary learning, and skill development.
• The review aimed to identify bottlenecks and accelerate the rollout of NEP initiatives across states and educational institutions.
gsSubTopics: Development processes and the development industry —the role of NGOs, SHGs, various groups and associations, donors, charities, institutional and other stakeholders.
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
prelimsPoints: The National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 replaced the 1986 policy.
It proposes significant structural changes from school to higher education.
Key pillars include access, equity, quality, affordability, and accountability.
It recommends a 5+3+3+4 curricular structure for school education.
Emphasis on multidisciplinary learning, critical thinking, and vocational education.
mainsPoints: Critically evaluate the key reforms proposed by the National Education Policy 2020 and their potential to transform India's education system.
Discuss the challenges encountered in the implementation of NEP 2020, particularly concerning federal cooperation, funding, and teacher preparedness.
Analyze how NEP 2020 aims to align India's education system with the demands of the 21st-century knowledge economy and global standards.
GS2.imageDescription: Students learning in a modern classroom setting, symbolizing educational reform.
GS2.trivia: NEP 2020 aims to increase the Gross Enrolment Ratio (GER) in higher education to 50% by 2035.

GS3.headline: India's Economy Registers Robust Growth in Q1 FY25, Driven by Manufacturing and Services
GS3.explainer:
• India's Gross Domestic Product (GDP) showed strong performance in the first quarter of Fiscal Year 2024-25.
• The growth was primarily propelled by robust activity in the manufacturing and services sectors.
• This positive trend indicates resilience in the domestic economy amidst global uncertainties.
• Government policies and increased domestic demand are cited as key contributors to this economic expansion.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Inclusive growth and issues arising from it.
Government Budgeting.

Major crops cropping patterns in various parts of the country, different types of irrigation and irrigation system storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers.
GS3.source: Ministry of Statistics and Programme Implementation (Simulated)
prelimsPoints: Q1 FY25 refers to the period from April to June 2024.
GDP measures the total monetary or market value of all the finished goods and services produced within a country's borders in a specific time period.
Manufacturing and services are typically the largest contributors to India's GDP.
High GDP growth indicates economic expansion and potential for job creation.
mainsPoints: Analyze the key factors contributing to India's robust economic growth in Q1 FY25, particularly highlighting the roles of manufacturing and services.
Discuss the challenges and opportunities for sustaining this growth trajectory in the medium to long term, considering global economic headwinds.
Evaluate the effectiveness of current government policies in fostering economic growth and ensuring inclusive development.
GS3.imageDescription: Upward trending graph showing India's GDP growth, with symbols representing industry and services.
GS3.trivia: India is projected to be one of the fastest-growing major economies globally in the current decade.

GS3.headline: Government Launches New Scheme to Boost Green Hydrogen Production and Adoption
GS3.explainer:
• The Indian government has unveiled a comprehensive new scheme aimed at accelerating green hydrogen production and its adoption across various sectors.
• The initiative includes incentives for manufacturing electrolysers and producing green hydrogen, alongside demand creation mechanisms.
• This move is part of India's broader National Green Hydrogen Mission, targeting energy independence and decarbonization.
• The scheme is expected to attract significant investments and create job opportunities in the renewable energy sector.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Infrastructure: Energy.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Investment models.
GS3.source: Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (Simulated)
prelimsPoints: Green hydrogen is produced by electrolysis of water using renewable energy sources.
India launched the National Green Hydrogen Mission in January 2023.
The mission aims to make India a global hub for green hydrogen production and export.
Electrolysers are key components for producing green hydrogen.
Applications include industrial feedstock, transportation fuel, and energy storage.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of green hydrogen for India's energy security and climate change mitigation goals.
Analyze the key components and expected outcomes of the government's new scheme to boost green hydrogen production and adoption.
Identify the challenges in scaling up green hydrogen production and infrastructure in India and suggest policy measures to overcome them.
GS3.imageDescription: A futuristic image of an electrolyser plant producing green hydrogen with wind turbines and solar panels in the background.
GS3.trivia: India aims to achieve an annual green hydrogen production capacity of 5 million metric tonnes by 2030 under its National Green Hydrogen Mission.

GS3.headline: ISRO Prepares for Venus Orbiter Mission 'Shukrayaan-1', Aims for 2028 Launch
GS3.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) is actively preparing for its ambitious Venus Orbiter Mission, named 'Shukrayaan-1'.
• The mission aims to study the atmosphere and surface of Venus, focusing on its geological and atmospheric evolution.
• 'Shukrayaan-1' is tentatively scheduled for a 2028 launch, following extensive developmental work on scientific payloads.
• This mission signifies India's expanding capabilities in planetary exploration beyond Mars and the Moon.
gsSubTopics: Achievements of Indians in science & technology; Indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Awareness in the fields of Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights.
prelimsPoints: ISRO's Venus mission is named 'Shukrayaan-1'.
The primary objective is to study Venus's atmosphere and surface.
Venus is often called Earth's 'sister planet' due to similar size and mass, but has a vastly different atmosphere.
The mission will carry scientific payloads developed by Indian and international institutions.
Previous successful planetary missions by ISRO include Chandrayaan (Moon) and Mangalyaan (Mars).
mainsPoints: Discuss the scientific objectives of ISRO's 'Shukrayaan-1' mission and its potential contributions to understanding planetary evolution.
Analyze the challenges involved in a Venus mission, considering the planet's extreme atmospheric conditions.
Evaluate the significance of India's expanding space exploration program for its scientific capabilities, technological advancements, and international standing.
GS3.imageDescription: Artistic rendering of the Shukrayaan-1 orbiter approaching Venus, with its thick atmosphere visible.
GS3.trivia: Venus has the hottest surface temperature of any planet in our solar system, primarily due to a runaway greenhouse effect caused by its dense carbon dioxide atmosphere.

GS3.headline: Gigi Ganguly’s 'Biopeculiar' Heightens Awareness of Climate-Change-Threatened Natural World
GS3.explainer:
• Gigi Ganguly’s collection of speculative fiction, 'Biopeculiar', explores themes of climate change and environmental degradation.
• The book uses imaginative narratives to heighten readers' awareness of the climate-change-threatened natural world.
• It delves into potential future scenarios and the impact of human actions on ecosystems and biodiversity.
• 'Biopeculiar' contributes to the growing genre of 'cli-fi' (climate fiction), urging reflection and action on environmental issues.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Disaster and disaster management.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
mainsPoints: Discuss the role of speculative fiction, such as 'Biopeculiar', in raising public awareness and promoting dialogue on critical environmental issues like climate change.
Analyze how literary works can effectively communicate the long-term impacts of human activities on the natural world and inspire sustainable practices.
Examine the concept of the Anthropocene and its implications for understanding the relationship between humanity and the Earth's ecosystems.
GS3.imageDescription: A surreal book cover depicting distorted natural elements, symbolizing environmental change.
GS3.trivia: The term 'Anthropocene' was popularized by Nobel laureate Paul Crutzen, suggesting a new geological epoch dominated by human activity.

GS3.headline: 'Stories for the Anthropocene': Reflecting on Humanity's Mark on Earth
GS3.explainer:
• This collection of stories explores themes related to the Anthropocene, the proposed geological epoch defined by human impact on Earth.
• It brings together narratives that reflect on environmental changes, ecological crises, and the future of human-nature interaction.
• The anthology serves as a literary response to the challenges posed by climate change and biodiversity loss.
• It encourages readers to critically examine humanity's responsibility towards the planet.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Disaster and disaster management.
mainsPoints: Discuss how the concept of the Anthropocene fundamentally alters our understanding of human-environment interactions and global challenges.
Analyze the role of literature and storytelling in disseminating complex scientific concepts like climate change and fostering public engagement.
Examine the ethical considerations and responsibilities that arise from humanity's significant impact on Earth's systems.
GS3.imageDescription: Abstract artwork depicting human figures interacting with a changing global landscape.
GS3.trivia: While the term 'Anthropocene' is widely used, it is not yet formally adopted as a geological epoch by the International Commission on Stratigraphy (ICS).

GS3.headline: GST Council Convenes to Discuss Rate Rationalization and Compliance Reforms
GS3.explainer:
• The Goods and Services Tax (GST) Council held a crucial meeting to address various aspects of the indirect tax regime.
• Key agenda items included rate rationalization for certain goods and services, aiming for simplification and efficiency.
• Discussions also focused on enhancing compliance mechanisms and leveraging technology for better tax administration.
• The council sought to balance revenue generation with promoting economic growth and easing the burden on businesses.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Government Budgeting.
Inclusive growth and issues arising from it.
prelimsPoints: The GST Council is a constitutional body (Article 279A).
It is chaired by the Union Finance Minister and includes state finance ministers.
The council makes recommendations on GST rates, exemptions, thresholds, and rules.
Rate rationalization aims to simplify the existing multi-slab GST structure.
Enhanced compliance measures include improved e-invoicing and data analytics.
mainsPoints: Evaluate the effectiveness of the GST regime in India since its implementation, particularly in terms of revenue buoyancy and ease of doing business.
Discuss the challenges associated with GST rate rationalization and the implications of proposed reforms on various sectors of the economy.
Analyze the role of the GST Council as a unique federal institution in resolving fiscal issues between the Union and States.
GS3.imageDescription: A gavel and tax documents on a table, symbolizing GST council decisions.
GS3.trivia: GST was implemented in India on July 1, 2017, as a comprehensive indirect tax reform, replacing multiple cascading taxes levied by the central and state governments.

GS3.headline: BCCI Finalizes New IPL Media Rights Deal, Signals Major Revenue Boost for Cricket
GS3.explainer:
• The Board of Control for Cricket in India (BCCI) has concluded a new round of media rights sales for the Indian Premier League (IPL).
• The deal, spanning several years, is expected to generate unprecedented revenue, making IPL one of the most lucrative sports leagues globally.
• This significant financial injection will further bolster the development of cricket infrastructure and talent in India.
• The new rights package covers both television and digital streaming, reflecting evolving media consumption patterns.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Inclusive growth and issues arising from it.
keyTerms: Board of Control for Cricket in India (BCCI)
prelimsPoints: The IPL is a professional Twenty20 cricket league in India.
BCCI is the governing body for cricket in India.
Media rights deals are a major source of revenue for sports leagues.
The IPL's valuation has consistently grown since its inception in 2008.
Digital streaming platforms are increasingly acquiring sports broadcasting rights.
mainsPoints: Analyze the economic impact of the Indian Premier League (IPL) on the Indian sports industry and broader economy, including revenue generation and employment.
Discuss how the evolving landscape of media rights, particularly the shift towards digital platforms, is reshaping the business model of major sports leagues.
Evaluate the role of sports leagues like IPL in promoting national identity, cultural engagement, and soft power for India on the global stage.
GS3.imageDescription: An IPL trophy alongside a blurred cricket stadium, symbolizing commercial success in sports.
GS3.trivia: The IPL is often cited as the world's richest cricket league and one of the most valuable sports properties globally.
